RAISE' 25: Accelerating Financial Services with AI

Global thought Leadership
RAISE' 25 featured keynote sessions
from leading global experts who shared
insights on the future of artificial
intelligence in financial services:

Peter Norvig, Director of
Engineering at Google and
Education Fellow at the Stanford
Institute for Human-Centred
AI, highlighted the pragmatic
utility of AI in credit scoring,
particularly for developing
markets. He noted that AI’s ability
to analyse unstructured data is a
game-changer for underwriting
customers who lack traditional
banking footprints.

RAISE' 25, LTF’s flagship AI conclave,
marked a significant milestone in
the organisation’s journey towards
becoming a risk-first, tech-first,
multi-product retail financier
of choice powered by artificial
intelligence. Held on November
7, 2025, in Mumbai, the second
edition of the event brought
together over 4,500 participants,
including global technology leaders,
policymakers, fintech innovators,
developers, and BFSI professionals.
Centred on the theme ‘Accelerating
Financial Services with AI’, the
conclave highlighted how artificial
intelligence is transitioning from
experimental pilots to enterprisescale deployment across the
financial services ecosystem
transforming underwriting, fraud
detection, portfolio monitoring,
customer engagement, and
operational efficiency.

212

Accelerated AI

portfolio monitoring, fraud detection, and customer servicing.

Advanced AI
Showcasing next-generation technologies including generative AI,

Strengthening a Scalable and Secure Technology Backbone

Data Privacy, Cybersecurity, and Digital Risk Management

As LTF advances its technology-driven retail strategy, strengthening its digital infrastructure and cybersecurity framework
remains critical to ensuring resilience, scalability, and secure service delivery. Your Company continues to invest in cloud
infrastructure, high-availability systems, and robust information security practices to support growing digital volumes while
safeguarding customer data.

Given the scale of customer data handled across the digital ecosystem, LTF has established a comprehensive data privacy
and cybersecurity framework to safeguard sensitive information, ensure regulatory compliance, and maintain operational
resilience. Oversight is provided through the Board-level IT Strategy Committee, IT Steering Committee, and Information
Security Committee, supported by enterprise-wide policies aligned with key regulatory requirements and global standards.

Key elements of this technology backbone include:

Key pillars of the framework include:

Cloud-First Infrastructure
~85% of applications have been
migrated to Google Cloud, with
100% of customer-facing platforms
hosted on the cloud. Auto-scaling and
on-demand provisioning enable the
platform to manage peak transaction
volumes efficiently while improving
deployment agility.

Data Privacy and Protection
Sensitive customer data is safeguarded
through end-to-end encryption,
role-based access controls and data
minimisation practices, supported
by Data Leakage Prevention (DLP)
mechanisms across endpoints and
communication channels.

Always-On Architecture and
Operational Resilience

Multi-Layered Cybersecurity
Framework

Strong Governance and
Regulatory Alignment

Privacy-by-Design Data
Handling

Advanced Cybersecurity
Infrastructure

A microservices-based architecture,
supported by real-time performance
monitoring and AI-driven anomaly
detection, enables predictive
maintenance and high system
availability, helping prevent potential
disruptions before they impact
operations.

LTF operates on a Zero-Trust security
architecture, supported by advanced
security solutions such as NextGeneration Firewalls, Network Access
Control, Web Application Firewalls,
Privileged Access Management,
Database Activity Monitoring, and
Cloud Access Security Brokers (CASB).
Continuous monitoring, AI-powered
threat detection and centralised patch
management strengthen protection
across systems.

LTF operates under a Master
Cybersecurity Policy and Data Privacy
Policy, aligned with regulatory
expectations from RBI and other
authorities as well as the Digital
Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act.
Its information security management
systems are certified under
ISO 27001:2022, ISO 27701:2019 for
privacy management and
ISO 20000-1:2018 for IT service
management.

Your Company follows principles of
data minimisation, role-based access,
and Zero-Trust authentication, ensuring
that sensitive information is accessed
strictly on a need-to-know basis. All
Personally Identifiable Information
(PII) is protected through end-to-end
encryption, both in transit and at rest.

Multiple security technologies
including Next-Generation Firewalls,
Network Access Control, Web
Application Firewalls, Privileged
Access Management, Database
Activity Monitoring, and Cloud Access
Security Brokers, protect systems
across network, application and cloud
environments. DLP solutions further
monitor and prevent unauthorised
data transfers.

Risk Monitoring and Incident
Management

Continuous Risk Monitoring and
Independent Audits

Incident Response and
Operational Resilience

Security Awareness
and Culture

Regular vulnerability assessments
and penetration testing (VAPT),
structured incident management
workflows and third-party cybersecurity
risk assessments help identify and
mitigate potential threats proactively.
Geo-redundant infrastructure and
periodic disaster recovery drills further
strengthen business continuity.

Regular vulnerability assessments,
penetration testing (VAPT) and
configuration reviews help identify and
mitigate risks. All critical third-party
vendors undergo formal cybersecurity
risk assessments to strengthen
supply-chain security.

Structured incident management
workflows, supported by cyber crisis
response protocols and geo-redundant
infrastructure, ensure rapid threat
containment and business continuity.
Your Company also maintains cyber risk
insurance to mitigate potential financial
and operational impacts.

Employees undergo mandatory
cybersecurity and data privacy
training, supported by periodic
phishing simulations and ongoing
awareness campaigns. Externally,
initiatives such as ‘Sachet with Sachet’
promote secure digital practices and
fraud awareness among customers.

These integrated controls have enabled LTF to maintain strong data security outcomes, with no identified customer data
breaches or IT incidents resulting in financial penalties during FY26.

H U M A N C A P I TA L

EMPOWERING TALENT.
TRANSFORMING THE
FUTURE.

At LTF, people are central to how the organisation grows, adapts and creates long-term value. This chapter
explores how your Company is building a workplace where talent is nurtured, performance is enabled and
individuals find meaning in their contributions.
LTF’s transformation is being driven as much by its people as by its strategy. Growth is not only measured
in scale, but also in how individuals evolve and contribute. As the organisation progresses, it is creating an
environment where ambition is supported by opportunity, and performance is balanced with care.
The approach reflects multiple dimensions of the employee experience. Teams collaborate with greater agility,
leaders enable with intent and everyday moments of support, recognition and learning strengthen a connected
workplace. The focus extends beyond programmes and structures to the lived experience of being part of LTF,
where trust is built, voices are valued and growth is both personal and shared.
This journey is anchored in a clear yet powerful idea: when people have space to grow, confidence to lead and
support to succeed, they become the driving force behind sustained performance and meaningful impact.

WHY
this matters
LTF’s transformation is increasingly defined by how effectively it builds capability, strengthens culture, and
enables its talent to grow alongside the business. As your Company progresses, the ability to build future-ready
capabilities, foster a performance-led culture, and strengthen leadership depth will be critical to sustaining
growth and delivering long-term value.
Your Company is building a learning, caring, and agile organisation where collaboration replaces hierarchy, credit discipline
sharpens decision-making, and technology powers execution. A matrix structure with empowered regional leadership is
accelerating outcomes while reinforcing accountability and transparency.
In an environment defined by agility, innovation, and execution, a skilled and empowered workforce is central to translating
your Company’s strategic ambition into consistent business outcomes.
Your Company adopts a structured, people-first approach, integrating capability building, performance management,
employee well-being, and inclusion into its organisational framework. Digital academic platforms, leadership development
programmes, and robust HR systems enable consistent talent development and engagement at scale.

A Listening-Led culture

The framework discussed in the table above is anchored in your Company’s guiding philosophy of the Experience C.U.R.V.E.
of Happiness, which continues to serve as the organisation’s cultural compass, guiding leadership behaviour, shaping
workplace practices, and enhancing employee experience.
Building on this foundation, your Company is deepening the integration of C.U.R.V.E. principles across talent, performance,
and engagement initiatives, with a sharper focus on measurable outcomes, workforce productivity, and employee well-being.

Your Company has institutionalised a structured listening framework to continuously capture employee feedback, strengthen
engagement, and enable data-driven decision-making. Moving beyond periodic surveys, this framework integrates multiple
listening channels across the employee lifecycle, ensuring that insights are timely, actionable, and embedded into business
and people strategies.
In FY26, your Company continued to leverage the Great Place to Work (GPTW) survey for the second consecutive year as a
key anchor within its listening architecture. This was complemented by pulse surveys and structured employee interactions
to capture real-time sentiment across functions and geographies. Participation increased significantly from 73% to 89%,
covering over 34,000 employees, reflecting growing trust in the feedback process.

Competency Framework
Building Future-Ready Capabilities
During the year, your Company institutionalised a structured Competency Framework to strengthen workforce
capability, enhance role clarity, and align individual performance with strategic priorities. Designed to support LTF’s
transition towards a Risk-first, Tech-first organisation, the framework defines the capabilities required across roles and
levels, enabling consistent and future-ready talent development.
The framework creates a unified foundation for hiring, performance management, leadership development, and
capability building. It ensures employees across roles and locations operate with shared expectations and standards.
Through this structured approach, your Company is strengthening leadership depth, enhancing workforce readiness,
and enabling consistent execution of its strategic ambition. While the C.U.R.V.E. philosophy shapes the employee
experience, the Competency Framework translates organisational expectations into measurable behaviours that guide
performance and delivery.

& Victories

Together, these elements strengthen employee experience, engagement, and performance, supporting sustained
productivity and long-term organisational growth.

Embedding Competencies into Everyday Work
To drive enterprise-wide adoption, LTF has focused on embedding competencies into everyday work environment through a
combination of structured interventions, engagement platforms, and simple yet high-visibility initiatives. Competency-themed
dartboards are installed across meeting centres and common areas, turning informal spaces into touchpoints for repeated
behavioural reinforcement.
This is complemented by gamified board experiences such as Ludo and Snakes & Ladders, an interactive digital learning
course accessible to all employees, curated comic strips highlighting expected behaviours, and videos reinforcing key
messages. Competency badges are also integrated into the Engagement platform, enabling recognition and peer visibility for
demonstrated behaviours.
Together, these initiatives reflect LTF’s belief that sustainable behavioural change requires continuous engagement, ensuring
competencies move beyond documentation.

Building Stronger Talent Pipelines

Role Appreciation Programme (RAP)
The RAP is a structured pre-joining experience designed for frontline officers. It was introduced to address early attrition by
improving role clarity, and ensuring informed commitment prior to onboarding.

Strengthening Employer Brand and Campus Connect
To build a strong and sustainable early-career talent pipeline, your Company continues to strengthen its employer brand
through the Campus Connect programme. The initiative engages premier institutions through a structured approach,
including focused campus benchmarking and tiering, customised assessments, surveys, and a standardised, high-quality
selection process.
New hires benefit from a well-designed induction programme that accelerates role familiarisation and readiness, ensuring
alignment with organisational expectations. LTF’s flagship RAISE AI conclave further enhances your Company’s brand
presence in the BFSI ecosystem, helping attract top-tier talent and reinforcing its positioning as a future-ready employer.

Challenge Right
Your Company’s transformation is increasingly shaped by employees who are encouraged to question existing
approaches and improve outcomes. Curiosity and constructive challenge are becoming integral to how work gets done.
The focus is on strengthening problem-solving capabilities and encouraging cross-functional collaboration. It promotes
ownership and enables employees to contribute ideas that enhance performance and customer experience.
As part of this shift, LTF continues to embed digital, technology and AI-enabled ways of working across functions.
Experimentation is actively supported, productivity is strengthened through automation, and data-driven decisionmaking is becoming the norm.

internal communication and enablement

employees with role-specific AI use cases
across functions, helping them integrate AI
into daily workflows while strengthening
productivity and analytical rigour.

Ensuring Safe, Ethical and
Empowering Workplaces

5

Support Right
Your Company’s people approach is guided by the need to create an environment where employees feel secure,
safe, and valued. Alongside capability and performance, LTF recognises the importance of systems and governance
that prioritise workplace practices, inclusion, wellbeing, and safety. This approach integrates diversity and inclusion,
engagement listening, workplace safety, holistic health, and comprehensive benefits, enabling individuals to grow
with confidence and contribute meaningfully to LTF’s long-term success.
As part of this approach, LTF continues to strengthen frameworks and policies that safeguard employee rights
and promote fair and responsible practices across operations and the value chain. Human rights commitments are
aligned with the UN Guiding Principles for Business and Human Rights and the ILO’s Declaration on the Fundamental
Principles, and are anchored in your Company’s Human Rights Policy.
Your Company’s commitment to human rights extends across all operational offices, branches, and value chain
partners. It ensures compliance with applicable laws, including the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016.
Governance mechanisms are further strengthened through dedicated oversight, including the appointment of a liaison
officer. Your Company also ensures adherence to principles such as freedom of association, non-discrimination, and
the elimination of child and forced labour across its operations.

Vigil Mechanism/Whistleblower Framework
Enables confidential reporting of unethical practices,
with independent oversight and protection against
retaliation

operations. These efforts ensure that
employee concerns are addressed

to continuous improvement in

Advancing Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion
LTF’s approach to Diversity, Equity,
and Inclusion (DEI) has evolved from
a focused HR initiative into a strategic
business priority. Operating across
diverse geographies and customer
segments, your Company recognises
the need for a workforce that reflects
the communities it serves and
supports inclusive growth.
Over time, the DEI journey has
progressed from intent-led efforts
to a more structured, data-driven
approach. Efforts now span inclusive
hiring, workforce representation,
infrastructure readiness and employee
experience, ensuring diversity is
supported through both policy and
practice.
Non-Discrimination, Harassment,
and Equal Opportunity
Guided by its Diversity, Equity,
and Inclusion (DEI) Policy, LTF is
an equal opportunity employer
with zero tolerance for any kind
of discrimination. Your Company
maintains a safe working
environment and enforces a strict
policy against workplace and sexual
harassment.

Embedding DEI into
governance and business
strategy

A dedicated DEI Council, comprising
Management Committee members,
senior business leaders and
functional heads, provides strategic
direction and monitors progress.
It oversees implementation across
businesses and regions, ensuring
consistency and accountability.
The DEI Council works closely with
business leaders and functional
teams to ensure DEI priorities
are translated into business and
operations, driving ownership,
implementation, and sustained
outcomes.
DEI is no longer treated as a
standalone initiative. It is embedded
into leadership accountability and
business performance.

DEI metrics integrated into
leadership scorecards and
performance frameworks
Key diversity metrics, including
representation and progress
monitoring, linked to performance
and remuneration
This governance framework
marks a shift from programme-led
interventions to leadership-owned
outcomes.

A defining step in your
Company’s journey has been the
institutionalisation of DEI governance
mechanisms that embed inclusion
into business strategy and decisionmaking.

Reimagining
Women’s Participation in
Frontline Roles
ural Business Finance (RBF) has undergone a meaningful workforce transformation, with
LTF reshaping women’s participation in frontline field roles. Historically, these roles were
considered unsuitable for women due to safety, mobility, and infrastructure constraints.
Recognising that representation cannot improve through hiring targets alone, your
Company prioritised building the right enabling environment first.

Today, women represent 3,080 employees across a 41,301
employee workforce, reflecting sustained momentum as
your Company scales its frontline and field workforce.

Supporting life Transitions and Caregiving
Responsibilities
Your Company has introduced a range of policies to
support employees in balancing professional and personal
commitments across life stages:
Enhanced maternity and childcare leave provisions,
along with flexible work arrangements
Creche reimbursement for up to two children below six
years of age
Comfort Leave that allows women employees to take
up to two wellness leave days each month
Flexible work and work-from-home options available
during critical phases
These measures enable employees to better manage
caregiving, health and family responsibilities while
continuing to build their careers.

Policy interventions are complemented by workplace
infrastructure that further supports employees as they return
to work and manage personal responsibilities:
A dedicated Lactation Room at Head Office providing
privacy and hygiene for nursing mothers.
Local conveyance reimbursement and travel assistance for
expecting mothers.
Child attendant support during business travel.
Ergonomic workplace arrangements and safety measures.
Women Safety and Late-hour Cab Policy
7-Up Safety Advisory
Women-friendly Branch Safety Standards
Together, these initiatives reflect a shift towards life-stage
inclusion, recognising that employees’ needs evolve over time
and that sustained support drives retention, engagement, and
long-term career growth.

Culture Realisers
As your Company scales, embedding organisational values into everyday behaviours remains central to building a high-trust,
high-performance workplace. LTF launched the Culture Realizers initiative during the year to translate Company values into
consistent actions, encouraging employees and managers to demonstrate small but meaningful behaviours that strengthen
culture across teams.
Rather than relying only on top-down messaging, Culture Realizers promotes a visible, locally driven culture shaped through
everyday interactions, recognition and team conversations.

Four key interventions anchor this initiative:
Pat on the Back

Zero Hour

Joy Stop

LevelUp

platform that enables
employees to appreciate
colleagues in real time.

recognitions have
been shared across the
organisation, spanning
peer-to-peer, manager-topeer, and peer-to-manager
appreciation, reinforcing a
culture of recognition and
gratitude.

It is a dedicated space
for open, informal
conversations between
managers and their
teams, designed
to build trust and
encourage transparent
dialogue. The initiative
has facilitated more
than 50,000 team
conversations across
locations.

This is a wellbeing
initiative that encourages
employees to take
short breaks during the
workday to recharge.
The programme includes
simple wellness activities,
a one-question happiness
pulse check and weekly
wellbeing themes, such
as hydration walks, to
support employee energy
and wellbeing.

Under this initiative, senior

20+ training sessions,
equipping managers with
practical tools to give
and receive feedback
constructively and foster
continuous development
within teams.

Together, these initiatives embed recognition, open dialogue, wellbeing, and developmental feedback into everyday
interactions, helping bring LTF’s culture to life across the organisation.

Strengthening managerial capability
remains a key priority as LTF scales, with
managers playing a critical role in driving
performance, engagement and culture.
Insights from LTF’s Manager Scorecards
informed efforts to strengthen
managerial effectiveness and enable
focused development across teams. The
analysis identified three priority areas:
Strengthening leadership behaviours
demonstrated by managers
Building stronger manager–team
connect and trust
Enhancing managers’ ability to

Guided by these insights, LTF
introduced CREW2, a focused

Experiential Learning
Approach
CREW2 adopts an experiential,
scenario-driven learning model,
enabling managers to engage with
real situations and reflect on leadership
practices. The programme emphasises
practical tools and behavioural
interventions to strengthen trust,
improve communication and enhance
team leadership.

Measurable Impact

Enabling managers to build capability
and confidence within their teams

The programme has delivered
encouraging outcomes. Manager
scorecard ratings improved from
3.64 to 4.17, indicating stronger
leadership behaviours and improved
team engagement. Attrition among
participating managers declined
significantly from 26% to 9%,
reflecting improved managerial
effectiveness and retention.

As LTF expands across geographies, enabling employees to connect beyond their day-to-day roles remains essential to
strengthening engagement and culture. Sports and wellness initiatives create opportunities to collaborate across functions,

LTF fosters an open, fair, and respectful workplace where employees can confidently raise concerns and report misconduct. A
structured grievance redressal framework ensures concerns are addressed promptly, confidentially, and fairly.

Building Safer Workplaces through Systems and Oversight
A safe and healthy workplace is central to employee well-being, operational continuity and long-term organisational
resilience. LTF’s Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) approach extends beyond regulatory compliance, reflecting a proactive
commitment to employee safety across offices, branches, and frontline operations.
As your Company expands its branch footprint and field workforce, the OHS framework has evolved into a structured, systemdriven and preventive approach anchored in strong governance, standardisation, and active employee participation.

A System-led Approach to
Workplace Safety
LTF is committed to conducting
business safely, guided by its Health &
Safety Policy applicable across offices
and branches. OHS management
during the year was strengthened
through defined governance structures,
aligned with global standards and
supported by a clear roadmap to scale
safety practices.

Expanding ISO 45001 Certification Coverage
Your Company continues to strengthen alignment with global occupational
health and safety standards through ISO 45001 certification. During the year,
certification was retained at the Head Office following surveillance audits and
extended to key branches-Ahmedabad and Bengaluru.
All certified locations reported zero major or minor non-conformities, reflecting
robust safety systems and strong adherence to established standards. A clear
roadmap has been defined to progressively expand ISO 45001 certification
coverage across operations by FY27.
These efforts reflect a shift from location-specific compliance to a more
integrated, enterprise-wide safety framework.

Strengthening Employee Participation and
Preparedness

Emergency Preparedness
and Infrastructure

A key focus during the year was strengthening employee involvement in safety
practices and improving emergency preparedness.

LTF continues to strengthen
emergency readiness and workplace
safety infrastructure across locations.
Key measures include:

Enhanced Grievance Redressal for Safety
An enhanced digital grievance redressal mechanism was introduced, enabling
employees to:
Report safety concerns
Highlight workplace observations
Track resolution through a structured system
This initiative strengthens transparency and employee participation in workplace
safety.

Mandatory road safety training for all new field employees

Health & Wellness
Initiatives
LTF recognises that long-term
organisational performance is closely
linked to the physical, mental and
emotional wellbeing of its people
and their families. Accordingly, your
Company is strengthening a preventive
and holistic wellbeing ecosystem.
The focus is shifting from episodic
interventions to continuous, integrated
and everyday support.

Building a Proactive Health
Ecosystem
A major step in strengthening
employee healthcare during the year
was the adoption of dedicated wellness
partner, marking a shift from periodic
healthcare interventions to a more
continuous and preventive care model.
Traditionally, corporate healthcare
programmes relied heavily on annual
checkups and reactive consultations.
With the growing scale and geographic
spread of the workforce, LTF recognised
the need for a more connected and
ongoing healthcare experience that
supports employees beyond one-time
interactions.

Continuous engagement and personalised follow-ups support employees in
managing their health proactively over time.
Through continuous monitoring, HIRA audits and branch-level safety governance,
LTF tracks workplace incidents closely.
Work-related injuries:
There were zero high-consequence work-related injuries and zero fatalities as a
result of work-related injury at LTF premises.

Healthy Meal Initiative – Promoting Better Nutrition
Recognising the role of nutrition in long-term health and productivity, your
Company continued its Healthy Meal Initiative for employees in branches and
meeting centres. Employees in these locations often operate in remote or hightravel environments with limited access to nutritious food.
The initiative provides hygienic, home-cooked meals, reducing reliance on fast
food and encouraging healthier eating habits. It also creates opportunities for
colleagues to connect during shared meal breaks, strengthening team interaction
at branch locations.
Key benefits include:
Improved daily nutrition and energy levels
Greater convenience for branch and field employees
Opportunities for social interaction during meal breaks
The initiative has been scaled to over 1,279 branches across 12 states,
demonstrating its growing importance as part of everyday employee well-being.

The platform enables a continuous
care journey by providing:

Digital Emotional
Well-being Support
Employees are provided free access to
an emotional wellbeing app, enabling
confidential and accessible mental
health support.
The platform offers:
Confidential counselling services
Emotional wellbeing guidance
Support for stress, anxiety, and
personal challenges
This initiative enables employees to
seek support in a safe and private
manner, helping reduce stigma around
mental health.

Employee Remuneration and Benefits
As LTF continues to expand its workforce, a comprehensive and competitive
benefits framework remains central to attracting, retaining, and supporting
talent across life stages. Your Company’s approach combines financial security,
healthcare access, family support, and long-term wealth creation, ensuring
employees feel supported both professionally and personally.

Financial Security and Healthcare Protection
Employees are supported through a range of long-term financial and healthcare
benefits, including:
Retirement and Savings
Gratuity and Provident Fund contributions
Defined contribution plans supporting long-term financial stability
Insurance and Medical Coverage
Medical, accident and life insurance coverage
Flexibility to include family members
Group Mediclaim and digital healthcare access
Eligibility of a large proportion of employees for Employee State Insurance (ESI)
These benefits help strengthen financial resilience and provide security for
employees and their families.

Parental Leave and Return
to Work

enable them to balance professional
responsibilities with family
commitments. Your Company provides
parental leave benefits in line with
applicable regulations and internal HR
policies.

eligible employees may avail 26
weeks of maternity leave, up to 14
weeks of childcare leave, and up to
26 weeks of Work From Anywhere
(WFA), subject to eligibility criteria,
with an overall cap of one year on
time away from regular workplace
duties
Paternity leave: 5 days for eligible
employees
Additional support through
childcare benefits, flexible work
arrangements and reintegration
support for returning employees

leadership capability, and reinforce retention through structured, transparent people practices.
By aligning development with progression and linking growth to long-term engagement, your Company creates an

with hands-on exposure to multiple product lines, strengthening cross-functional understanding, and real-time decisionmaking.
Over time, the learning ecosystem has evolved from standalone training programmes to an integrated model supporting
ongoing skill development, leadership readiness, and role-based capability building.

Building Leadership
Depth

Maximising Potential

to move beyond role-based allocation
towards capability-based placement.

they create the greatest value.
This approach:
Encourages cross-functional
collaboration
Strengthens organisational agility
Builds a culture of ownership and
accountability

supports the creation of a more flexible
and resilient workforce.

strategy is role arbitrage, which enables
employees to take on expanded
responsibilities alongside their core
roles.
This practice helps:
Prepare employees for future
leadership roles
Strengthen organisational
continuity
Improve succession readiness
By encouraging multi-role exposure,

capable of adapting to changing

Awards spanned categories such as

At the Star Awards 2025 ceremony at
A. M. Naik Tower, Powai, your Company
honoured 195 employees from
1,791 eligible colleagues. They were
recognised for strong contributions and
for reflecting LTF’s values of Pride and

& CEO, attended alongside other

impact.

WHY
this matters
As LTF expands across geographies and customer
segments, strong relationships with customers, partners,
and communities remain central to sustaining longterm growth. In a rapidly evolving financial ecosystem,
transparent engagement, responsive action, and
inclusive solutions are critical to value creation. Access to
responsible financial services continues to play a vital role in
strengthening economic participation and resilience.
Your Company places customers, partners, and
communities at the core of its value creation framework,
recognising them as key drivers of sustainable growth.
It integrates stakeholder insights into decision-making
through structured feedback mechanisms, supported by
AI-led, data-driven intelligence. This enables proactive
risk management and strengthens service delivery. It also
ensures that products and solutions remain aligned with the
evolving needs of a diverse customer base, while fostering
long-term, trust-based relationships across the ecosystem.

Strengthening customer relationships through
responsible financial solutions and seamless service
delivery

and governance frameworks to enable informed
decision- making

HOW
LTF delivers
Your Company adopts a structured and integrated approach to stakeholder engagement, embedding stakeholder priorities
within its strategy and governance frameworks. This is supported by digital platforms, data-driven insights, and continuous
feedback mechanisms that enhance service delivery and accessibility.
Your Company strengthens its ecosystem by building employee capabilities to reinforce a customer-centric culture. It
collaborates with partners to expand distribution and operational efficiency. It also promotes responsible practices across
the value chain. Complemented by community initiatives that foster inclusion and resilience, this approach enables your
Company to deepen stakeholder relationships while advancing inclusive and sustainable growth.

Corporate Overview

Your Company has established structured governance mechanisms to ensure that customer feedback is translated into

“We have invited representatives
from various industries to share best
practices and experiences regarding
customer centricity”
Raju Dodti
Whole-time Director (Designate)
and Chief Operating Officer

Customer centricity means you
put the customer at the centre of
everything you do.
It is the art and science of delivering
a compelling value proposition that
addresses customers' ever-changing
needs, aiming for customer
relationships that build loyalty and
trust.
Your products, digital experiences,
and customer service at every
touch point should all be designed
to provide a consistently great
experience and add value.
Customer centricity should not
be restricted to one team; it is a
collaborative effort of all functions
across the organization.
Today, customers are no longer
just a number or a name in the
database. Instead, they are an
empowered, connected, and
engaged set of people.
Customers now expect more from
everyone, and they are increasingly
getting vocal about it. It is needless
to say that the way companies
interact with customers is becoming
crucial, and the overall customer

260

experience now dictates where they
want to spend their money and
develop loyalty.
The customer experience, thus,
becomes the primary differentiator in
a sea of sameness and has a voice in
your Company’s direction.
A customer-centric approach is
essential in a company like ours,
which has over 2.8 Cr+ customers.
Studies suggest that one in three
customers tends to leave the brand
they love after having one bad
experience.
At LTF, in our journey to establish a
robust retail portfolio with quality
assets, we are shifting the emphasis
from a product-centric to a customercentric company.
This will benefit us in numerous
ways, including increasing customer
loyalty, operating efficiency, and
generating more business and
revenue.
To reflect our commitment, the
“Customer Service” team has
been renamed as the “Customer
Experience Team”. Additionally, we
have undertaken initiatives to foster
a "Customer-Centric Culture" across
the organization.

Safeguarding Customer Trust through Data Privacy and Cybersecurity
In an increasingly digital and financial ecosystem, protecting customer data is fundamental to maintaining and strengthening
stakeholder trust. To safeguard sensitive customer information and ensure continuous operational resilience, your Company
has received ISO 27701 certification, an international, accredited standard for managing Personally Identifiable Information
(PII) acting as an extension to ISO 27001(June 2025). Your company is also in process of implementing the Digital Personal
Data Protection Act, 2023 in adherence to provisions of DPDP Rules 2025.

16,794

13,877

No. of Complaints FY25

No. of Complaints FY26

Reduction in Complaints

Demonstrating strong regulatory compliance, all customer complaints are addressed within the stipulated turnaround time as
prescribed by regulators, with most cases resolved well within defined timelines. Initiatives such as Project Seven has further
enhanced responsiveness by streamlining grievance redressal mechanisms, enabling faster resolution, improved tracking and
greater transparency for customers.

Governance through the Stakeholders Relationship and Customer
Protection Committee
To ensure governance of customer-centric initiatives and stakeholder interests, your Company operates a dedicated Boardlevel Stakeholder Relationship and Customer Protection Committee. This Committee provides strategic oversight over
customer service standards, grievance redressal frameworks, and comprehensive consumer protection mechanisms. The
Committee actively reviews insights generated from the grievance redressal and I.O frameworks to identify any underlying
issues. The governance standard is further strengthened by the independent Internal Ombudsman briefing the Committee
on the cases reviewed by the them and various process enhancements carried out to the existing processes.
For more details, refer to section 'Corporate Governance'.

Designing Solutions around Customer Needs

Your Company promotes responsible borrowing through clear and transparent communication across its branch networks
and through digital platforms, and marketing channels. It outlines product features, pricing, repayment obligations,
and customer rights in a simple and accessible manner. This approach aligns with regulatory requirements and internal
governance standards, while strengthening customer understanding and trust.
Digital platforms and financial awareness initiatives further support informed decision-making. The PLANET App ecosystem,
financial literacy programmes, and fraud awareness campaigns enable customers to better understand financial products,
credit responsibilities, and digital safety practices.

Enhancing Customer Access through Multi-Product Sampoorna
Branches
To improve accessibility and convenience for customers, your Company has introduced multi-product Sampoorna branches.
These branches offer a comprehensive suite of financial solutions through a single touchpoint. The model enables customers
to seamlessly access multiple products, enhancing engagement, and simplifying their financial journeys.
In FY26, LTF launched its first Sampoorna branch in Ujjain, Madhya Pradesh, and expanded the network to 30+ locations.
These branches strengthen LTF’s ability to serve diverse customer segments, particularly in emerging markets, thereby
supporting financial inclusion and deeper customer relationships.

through Lakshya 2031 with a focus on leveraging technology, data and AI to strengthen customer engagement, and improve
service efficiency.

regulatory compliance.

Transparent Grievance Redressal
Mechanism
At LTF, ensuring exceptional customer satisfaction
is foundational to its operations. Your Company
has established a structured, multi-tiered Grievance
Redressal framework to ensure customer complaints are
acknowledged and resolved in a fair and timely manner.
LTF has placed customer satisfaction at the core of its
operations, supported by a comprehensive grievance
redressal framework. Dedicated grievance teams are
established to manage a clearly defined escalation path,
where the customer can initially approach Level 1, then
moving to the escalation channels, Grievance Redressal
Office & Principal Nodal Office and finally may approach
the Regulator. This approach ensures accountability at
every stage while delivering the resolution and structured
mechanism for addressing the customer concerns.

268

Integrated Annual Report 2025-26

LTF’s Digital Ecosystem
PLANET App (PLANET 3.0)
Target Audience: Retail Customers

Internal Targets for Resolution Timelines
compared to the 30-day regulatory requirement

Technology-enabled Tracking and
Escalation
Every customer interaction is meticulously logged and
tracked, automatically categorising and routing complaints
to centralised resolution teams. If an issue remains
unresolved at the initial touchpoint, a strictly defined
escalation matrix ensures that it is systematically escalated
to the Grievance Redressal Officer (GRO), the Principal Nodal
Officer, and ultimately to the regulator as appropriate.

Ensuring Absolute Fairness
To uphold the highest standards of equity and unbiased
resolution, LTF has appointed an Internal Ombudsman in
accordance with RBI directives. This independent authority
rigorously reviews all customer complaints that are either
partially or wholly rejected by your Company, ensuring
fairness and regulatory compliance across the Board.

Omnichannel Accessibility and AI-Driven
Resolutions
Recognising the need for seamless accessibility, your
Company offers a wide spectrum of physical and digital
touchpoints for raising grievances. Customers can easily
connect through branch walk-ins, dedicated helplines,
emails, and website submissions. Concurrently, digitalfirst solutions such as the PLANET App and the AIpowered multilingual conversational assistant, KAI, have
fundamentally transformed query management. These
advanced self-service platforms empower customers with
real-time, instantaneous resolutions, significantly enhancing
the customer experience.

Embedding Sustainability
Practices across the Value Chain

Sustainability Value Chain
Assessment

LTF also supports businesses within its ecosystem through
targeted financing solutions designed to address working
capital needs and strengthen financial resilience.

Responsible value chain engagement remains central
to LTF’s sustainability journey. Your Company promotes
ethical conduct, environmental responsibility and social
accountability across its ecosystem through its Third-Party
Code of Conduct (TPCoC) applicable to vendors, suppliers,
dealers, and service providers.

To drive efficiency, and seamless tracking across partner
ecosystem, LTF has successfully automated its value
chain assessments. In FY26, your Company deployed
a dedicated Value Chain Assessment Tool, marking a
strategic transition from periodic manual reviews to realtime, automated monitoring of its supply chain.

To strengthen ESG alignment, LTF conducts structured
value chain assessments covering a significant share of
upstream and downstream partners by value. These
assessments evaluate performance of suppliers and
partners across environmental, social, and governance
parameters, supporting responsible procurement, and
sustainable business practices.

Utilising this automated tool, LTF conducted structured
Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG)
assessments covering the top 75% of its value chain
partners by value.

Key focus areas include:

Specifically, the tool evaluates the overall ESG maturity of
partners by focusing on the following key parameters:

Through its SME Finance franchise, your Company
offers:
Supply chain financing solutions to support corporate
ecosystems

	Open API infrastructure enabling seamless platform
integrations

I

India’s digital public
infrastructure has significantly
expanded access to banking
and government services.
However, access alone does
not guarantee meaningful
participation or long-term
financial resilience, particularly
in rural hinterlands.
Recognising this gap, your Company
views digital and financial inclusion
as a last-mile intervention that
creates knowledge and awareness,
enables informed decision-making,
and supports households, especially
women, through economic transitions.
This is especially relevant for rural
women, who often manage household
finances but remain excluded from
the formal financial ecosystem. Over
time, this approach has evolved from
enabling access to building capability
and, increasingly, strengthening
resilience, with Digital Sakhi emerging
as your Company’s CSR flagship
program for women-led digital and
financial empowerment.

Digital Sakhi:
A Women-led Programme
The Digital Sakhi programme was launched in FY18 as a
community-driven, women-led initiative to advance digital and
financial literacy. Initially piloted in Maharashtra, its strong onground impact led to expansion across Madhya Pradesh, Tamil
Nadu, West Bengal, and Odisha in the first phase. Building on
the success and learnings of this model, in FY25, Digital Sakhi 2.0
emerged as an evolved, focussing more on leveraging technology
and scalable version, deepening impact through enhanced capacity
building and ecosystem integration.
With expansion in Karnataka and Kerala, marking a strategic shift
from access and awareness to outcome-driven digital inclusion.
Insights from earlier phases highlighted the need for deeper
engagement, particularly around cyber safety, enterprise readiness,
and real-time monitoring. The programme was further extended
in Tamil Nadu, West Bengal, Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, Rajasthan
(FY24 & FY25) and Gujarat (FY26).
The programme is designed across three core components. The
first focuses on the identification and capacity building of Digital
Sakhis, equipping women with digital and financial knowledge,
tools, and leadership skills. The second component centres on
women’s entrepreneurship, in which existing and aspiring women
entrepreneurs are identified, trained, and supported throughout
their enterprise journey. The third component positions Digital
Sakhis as community catalysts, enabling last-mile access to
government schemes and entitlements by bringing services directly
to rural households.
To ensure scale, sustainability, and convergence, the programme
is implemented in partnership with like-minded implementation
agencies and supported by active collaboration with Government
stakeholders, creating a robust and inclusive ecosystem for
community transformation.

Midline and endline assessments

in Karnataka

At the heart of our Digital Sakhi initiative, one of the key components is the efficient delivery of Government
Social Entitlement Schemes to the last mile. By bridging the gap between underserved communities and statesponsored benefits, we ensure our interventions have a multiplier effect on social welfare.

In Karnataka project areas, the initiative
achieved 100% SIP saturation across
200+ villages, ensuring every household
is covered under at least one financial
entitlement.

Corporate Overview

SUCCESS STORIES

Extended Impact
Going Beyond Digital and Financial Inclusion
From Farmer to State-Recognised Entrepreneur
Ranjana Yadav from Unaula Doyam, Gorakhpur, transformed her livelihood
after joining the Digital Sakhi Project. Leveraging entrepreneurship training, she
shifted to medicinal plant cultivation, increasing her income through market
linkages. She emerged as a local leader, sharing her learnings and representing
her district at the Kisan Medicinal Fair, Lucknow. Recognised for her impact,
she was honoured by Honourable Chief Minister of Uttar Pradesh, becoming a
symbol of rural women-led agri-entrepreneurship.

Atta Chakki on Wheels: Mobile Enterprise
Sangeeta Devi from Ghata Mishra, Kushinagar, transformed her livelihood
through Digital Sakhi-linked EDP training. Identifying the lack of local milling
services, she took a ₹45,000 loan to launch an innovative mobile flour mill
mounted on a tractor, serving multiple villages. Her enterprise now ensures
steady income of ₹25,000/- per month. In a significant milestone, she funded
her daughter’s marriage entirely through her own earnings, becoming a strong
example of rural women-led entrepreneurship and self-reliance.

From Local Venture to Award-Winning Enterprise
Shakthivel Food Products, a women-led enterprise nurtured under the Digital
Sakhi programme in South Karnataka, showcases the power of structured
entrepreneurship support. Through targeted training, the team strengthened
capabilities in business management, quality control, and market expansion.
Their efforts were recognised at the International Women Entrepreneurship Day
in Bengaluru, where they received the Best Enterprise Award from FKCCI,
presented by Minister Lakshmi Hebbalkar. Emerging from the Kolar clusters,
the enterprise reflects the programme’s sustainability model, transforming
local initiatives into scalable, high-impact businesses and inspiring women-led
entrepreneurship.

IV

Disaster
Management
Standing with Communities in
Times of Crisis
Climate change continues to
intensify the frequency and severity
of natural disasters, disrupting lives,
livelihoods, and local economies
across India. In particular, floods,
droughts, and extreme heat events
disproportionately affect rural and
low-income communities, exposing
families to sudden losses of shelter,
income, and stability.
Against this backdrop, Disaster
Management forms an integral part of
LTF’s CSR and risk framework. In FY26,
your Company further deepened its
approach, strengthening integration,
governance, and a long-term
resilience orientation.
FY26 was influenced by the residual
effects of severe climate events
experienced in the previous year. As
a result, these conditions required
calibrated, geography-specific
interventions, particularly in floodand heat-affected agricultural belts
where vulnerable households faced
heightened income and livelihood
stress.

Fast track Response: Relief, Risk and Recovery
LTF’s Disaster Management framework is built on the
understanding that timely relief and financial resilience must
operate in tandem. On the ground, your Company partners with
implementing agencies to deliver immediate relief, enabling rapid
response in disaster-affected regions.

On-Ground Relief and Community Support
In response, your Company worked closely with CSR
implementing agencies, local administrations, and communitybased organisations to implement targeted disaster response
interventions across affected districts in Bihar, Uttar Pradesh,
Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan, and Odisha. Operating amid
waterlogged terrain, disrupted transport networks, and
constrained last-mile access, field teams focused on delivering
timely relief while adapting to evolving ground conditions.
Key areas of support included:
Distribution of essential relief kits to flood- and heat-affected
households
Assistance to families impacted by extreme weather events,
including floods, heatwaves, and storms

From Climate Risk to
Rural Resilience
Climate risk is increasingly shaping
rural economic outcomes. For
institutions deeply engaged with
agrarian and rural ecosystems,
environmental stress, particularly
water scarcity and land degradation,
has emerged as a material factor
influencing livelihoods, productivity,
and long-term sustainability.
In response to these realities, your
Company positioned Climate Impact
Management in FY26 as a preventive
and resilience-building strategy,
addressing climate risk at its source.
By investing in water conservation
and management, soil restoration,
and climate-smart agriculture, your
Company seeks to reduce vulnerability
while enabling durable economic
participation in climate-sensitive
regions.

Financial Statements

Jalvaibhav 2.0: Water Security as
the Foundation

IV

Climate Impact
Management

Statutory Reports

Our Climate Response
LTF’s climate strategy focuses on practical, field-led interventions
that deliver both environmental and economic outcomes. Rather
than treating conservation as a standalone objective, initiatives
are designed to strengthen rural systems that directly support
livelihoods.

Core focus areas include
Integrated Water Resource Management (IWRM)

Building on earlier Integrated Water Resource Management
efforts, Jalvaibhav 2.0 expanded into new climate-vulnerable
regions in FY26, with water security positioned as the
anchor for resilience. The Jalvaibhav 2.0 project is extended
in the Valsad district in Gujarat and in the Umaria district
(Bandhavgarh) of Madhya Pradesh.

Jalvaibhav 2.0 – Valsad, Gujarat
Third-party need assessments and community participatory appraisals were conducted in 10 villages
Created 400 kL of water harvesting capacity, bringing 400 acres under irrigation
Installation of 5 solar irrigation units, providing 500+ farmers with sustainable energy

Agroforestry, horticulture plantation, and land restoration

Over 500 farmers received "Smart Agriculture" training, helping them adopt climate-resilient techniques to improve long-term
crop yields

Climate-smart agricultural and allied practices

Established 2 drinking water ATMs for community members and provided income-generating polyhouses for improving livelihood

Community-led stewardship of natural resources
Together, these interventions improve water availability, regenerate
degraded land, enhance biodiversity, and stabilise farm-based
incomes.
Since FY2016-17, your Company has been driving water
conservation in Marathwada region of Maharashtra by creating
harvesting structures and promoting water-efficient devices.
To ensure these benefits last, we empowered 120 Water User
Groups (WUGs), training over 500+ members, mostly small, and
marginalised farmers to manage maintenance, crop planning, and
user charges.

Solar Lift Irrigation

Transforming Agriculture in Kaprada, Valsad
In Kaprada, despite high annual rainfall, small and marginal
farmers have historically relied on single-season rainfed
kharif monocropping. The lack of irrigation infrastructure
prevented cultivation during the rabi season, forcing many
households to migrate in search of alternative livelihoods. To
address this, we installed five solar-based lift irrigation units,
bridging the gap between available local water sources such as rivers and check dams, and the farmers' fields.

Key Highlights
Expanded Infrastructure
Installed 5 solar lift irrigation units across 5 villages, bringing
approximately 90 acres of land under assured irrigation.
Economic Empowerment
119 small and marginal farmers are now equipped to
transition from single-season rainfed agriculture to
multiple-cropping cycles.
Crop Diversification
The shift has enabled farmers to cultivate high-value cash
crops and vegetables during the rabi season, boosting farm
income and reducing the need for seasonal migration.
Community-Led Sustainability
Established Water User Groups (WUGs) at each site to
ensure local ownership. With dedicated bank accounts and
an initial maintenance grant of ₹10,000 per group, the
community is empowered to manage operational costs and
repairs through collective fee collection, ensuring the longterm functionality of the solar infrastructure.

Through these interventions, more reliable access to water is being created for both agricultural and domestic needs, directly
supporting productivity and livelihood stability.

Corporate Overview

Jalvaibhav 2.0 – Bandhavgarh, Madhya Pradesh
In Bandhavgarh, the project created and renovated ponds in Bhardua and Guruwahi, adding over 11,000 kL storage.

Capacity building efforts reached over 1,000 participants, including farmers, women, and youth, through specialized
environmental education materials.
Local governance was established by forming four Water User Groups and Biodiversity Committees to manage
regional resources.
The initiative completed four dedicated training sessions to ensure effective community management of the new
water assets.

Project Prakruti: Building on Strong Foundations
LTF’s flagship environmental initiative, Project Prakruti, continued to anchor land restoration and biodiversity efforts,
providing a strong foundation.

Scale
Under Project Prakruti, a cumulative total of 2,68,000 plantations were successfully maintained throughout FY26, driving
significant environmental and local economic impact.

Karnataka Horticultural Drive
The initiative resulted in plantation of 2,00,000 horticulture saplings in Pavagada, Tumakuru.

Maharashtra Green Cover
Leveraging the efficient Miyawaki methodology, the project successfully established 68,000 saplings in Bhavthana, Pisegaon,
and Salegaon to enhance regional biodiversity and green cover.

Key Highlights

Across Karnataka (Tumakuru District) and Maharashtra (Beed and Dharashiv District)

Integrated Annual Report 2025-26

IV

Social
Inclusion
Enabling Dignity, Safety and
Opportunity
Even as India continues on a path
of economic expansion, access to
opportunity remains uneven. For large
sections of the population, particularly
rural youth, women, and underserved
communities, persistent barriers to
employability, preventive healthcare,
and personal safety continue to
restrict participation in growth.
In response, Social Inclusion at LTF is
approached as a human development
imperative. In FY26, health, and safety
were addressed not in isolation, but as
interconnected foundations of dignity,
resilience, and economic agency.
Guided by this perspective, your
Company's Social Inclusion strategy
focuses on addressing everyday
vulnerabilities through targeted
interventions that strengthen
individual capabilities and reinforce
community resilience.

The approach is anchored on:
Improving access to preventive healthcare in underserved regions
Enhancing road safety awareness and infrastructure to reduce
avoidable risk
Establishing the animal welfare centre to provide health care
services to the stray dogs from the region
By addressing these dimensions together, LTF aims to reduce structural
disadvantages and enable long-term social mobility.

Strengthening CSR through Third-Party
Stakeholder Engagement
In a first-of-its-kind initiative in the BFSI sector, we partnered with CRISIL to undertake a comprehensive third-party
stakeholder engagement study, reinforcing our commitment to transparency, inclusivity, and impact-driven CSR. This
exercise went beyond conventional assessments by systematically capturing insights from all key stakeholders, community
members, Digital Sakhis, implementation partners, local institutions, and internal teams.
The study enabled us to understand on-ground realities, expectations, and gaps across programme geographies. By
incorporating these diverse perspectives, we ensured that every stakeholder became an active participant in shaping the
CSR interventions, rather than just a beneficiary.
It also helped align interventions more closely with community needs, ensuring higher adoption, ownership, and long-term
sustainability.
This collaborative and evidence-led approach has significantly improved the effectiveness of programme execution on the
ground. By embedding stakeholder voices into decision-making, we have built a more responsive and resilient CSR model
setting a new benchmark for the BFSI sector in driving inclusive and participatory development.

WHY
this matters
As LTF advances its journey towards
Lakshya 2031, environmental
stewardship remains a core pillar of its
responsible growth strategy.
At LTF, environmental stewardship is
integral to how your Company scales its
operations. As your Company expands
its physical footprint and strengthens
its presence across geographies,
environmental factors play a critical role
in ensuring efficient resource utilisation,
operational resilience and value creation.
During the year, your Company has
significantly expanded its pan-India
footprint, including the strategic
acquisition and scaling of its gold finance
business, further deepening its reach
across diverse geographies and customer
segments. As operations expand, so does
your Company’s environmental footprint
making it imperative to proactively
embed sustainability into business
growth.

WHAT
we focus on
During this phase of accelerated growth, LTF is strengthening environmental
management across both operations and portfolios, with a clear focus on
efficiency, oversight and climate risk preparedness.

Building on its legacy of enabling
inclusive development, your Company
has strengthened its commitment to
climate action, resource efficiency, and
nature-positive impact embedding
environmental sustainability into both
operations and financing decisions
Over the years, your Company has
evolved from a progressive approach to
a more data-driven and transformative
sustainability framework, guided by
Board oversight and cross-functional
ownership. LTF has implemented
double materiality assessments, climate
risk evaluations, and third-party
stakeholder engagements, ensuring that
environmental priorities are aligned with
both business resilience and societal
expectations.

Together, robust governance mechanisms and technology-enabled execution provide a scalable and disciplined framework
for delivering your Company’s environmental agenda. Environmental governance is further strengthened through
independent reasonable and limited assurance engagements for sustainability reporting. These processes are supplemented
by the use of external experts assessing performance, validating key metrics, and conducting targeted sustainability studies.

Business Impact Study
Environmental Value Ledger
LTF’s environmental performance is assessed through
the Business Impact Study (BIS), evaluating the societal
value created through business activities. Using the
Social Cost of Carbon methodology, the study translates
certain climate-related outcomes into economic value.
It accounts for emissions avoided through EV financing
and emissions associated with financed portfolios. This
contextualises environmental performance not only in
physical terms (tCO2e, energy use, water consumption)
but also through the lens of societal costs and benefits,
strengthening your Company’s understanding of longterm environmental value creation.

Environmental Responsibility Inside and Outside the Fence

Internal Carbon Pricing

Our Net-Zero Approach

Your Company is committed to minimising the environmental footprint of its nationwide operations through energy
efficiency, digitisation, and circularity.

As part of Your Company’s commitment to managing
climate-related risks and facilitating investments in lowcarbon initiatives, LTF has evaluated Internal Carbon Pricing
(ICP) mechanisms to guide its decarbonization journey.
The Gross ICP has been calculated using combined capital
and operational expenditures (INR per tCO2e) across your
Company’s Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions and will be used
for internal purposes.

In FY26, your company has identified several levers
across its Scope 1, Scope 2, Scope 3, and Financed
Emissions to reduce the same. These strategies
broadly encompass enhancing the energy efficiency
and renewable energy mix of our physical operations,
advancing sustainable digital workflows, and crucially,
integrating climate-smart, low-carbon solutions across
our core lending portfolios to support our customers'
green transitions. Through these proactive measures,
our organization is in line with continuous emission
reductions which aligns with a Net-Zero approach,
even though Net Zero has not been taken as a formal
target and the same is under systematic evaluation.

Some of the key initiatives include:

Looking beyond its operational footprint, your Company is
also exploring the application of a Shadow Price to the LTF
lending portfolio. As this practice continues to evolve in the
Indian NBFC market, your Company intends to incorporate
ICP into its strategic decision-making as policy clarity
develops.

Climate Risk
Over the course of the year, the rising
frequency and severity of natural
disasters, including severe floods,
cyclones, prolonged heatwaves, and
droughts, have further underscored
the urgency of climate action.
These events are no longer isolated
disruptions; they represent systemic
risks with cascading economic
and social consequences. Against
this backdrop, Your Company
remains cognisant of the growing
environmental challenges arising
from climate change, including
extreme weather events, rising
sea levels, and desertification. The
environmental costs continue to
escalate, with developing nations
projected to face annual losses of
US$ 447–894 billion by 2030. For
businesses, operational disruptions,
and transition risks, such as evolving
regulations and carbon pricing, are

reshaping markets and redefining
business models. In this evolving
landscape, your Company continues
to recognise the critical role financial
institutions play in enabling a lowcarbon, climate-resilient future.
Your Company’s climate action
strategy has continued to advance
into a comprehensive and
forward-looking approach, with
environmental considerations
systematically integrated across
its operations. With an increased
focus on climate resilience, your
Company is strengthening its
business portfolio while delivering
long-term, sustainable value for
stakeholders. Sustainability-driven
initiatives are incorporated not only
within products and services but also
across core operations, with a strong

emphasis on resource efficiency
and operational sustainability.
This holistic integration ensures
that sustainability remains central
to decision-making, fostering a
culture that is adaptive, forwardlooking, and responsive to evolving
environmental realities.
Your Company’s climate action
framework continues to be anchored
by clearly defined targets, including
achieving carbon neutrality by
2035 and maintaining water
positive or surplus operations.
These commitments guide
strategic planning, and stakeholder
engagement, reinforcing Your
Company’s long-term value creation
agenda while aligning with broader
environmental priorities.

Integrating Climate
Risks into Business
Operations
Climate-related risks have been
integrated more deeply into the
Risk Management Framework
as an emerging risk and Early
Warning Signals, strengthening
your Company’s ability to anticipate,
monitor, and mitigate emerging risks.

Managing Physical
and Transition Risks
Physical risks, including extreme
weather events such as floods,
droughts, and cyclones, are actively
incorporated within your Company’s
emerging risk management
processes. Your Company undertakes
detailed assessments to anticipate
potential disruptions and ensure
operational continuity. These risks
are evaluated not only in terms of
immediate impact but also for their
longer-term implications on borrower
resilience.

Climate Risk Assessment in Portfolios
LTF applies a robust and data-driven approach to assess climate risks across its
key portfolios, including Urban Finance, SME Finance, and Rural Finance. Tools
and analytical models are deployed to evaluate climatic variables such as rainfall
variability, irrigation coverage, and temperature fluctuations.
In FY26, your Company transitioned to district-level assessment granularity,
marking a decisive shift towards precision-led climate risk management. This
enhanced level of detail enables your Company to move beyond generalized risk
indicators and capture location-specific climate exposures with greater accuracy.

Identifying Climate-Related Opportunities
LTF increasingly approaches climate change as both a risk and a driver of
innovation and sustainable growth. Your Company has actively identified
opportunities aligned with the transition to a low-emission economy, particularly
in areas such as electric vehicle financing, which supports the shift away from
fossil fuel-based transportation. By aligning with regulatory developments and
market shifts, Your Company continues to expand its green finance portfolio.

During FY26, your Company
expanded and refined its assessment
of transition risks by incorporating
a structured evaluation of policy
and legal risks, technology risks,
market and reputational risks, and
governance-related considerations.

Climate Scenario Analysis

Governance and
Oversight of Climate
Risks
A robust governance structure
underpins Your Company’s approach
to climate risk management.
Oversight is exercised through
the Board and the CSR and
Sustainability Committee, ensuring
that sustainability considerations are
built into strategic and operational
decision-making. The Chief
Economist regularly updates the
Board on environmental and climatic
parameters, enabling informed
oversight.
Your Company also undertakes
periodic third-party assessments,
audits, and scenario analyses
to strengthen transparency,
compliance, and the overall
robustness of its climate risk
approach. Climate considerations
are increasingly aligned with
enterprise-wide governance
processes, supported by ongoing
efforts to enhance internal
capabilities and deepen climaterelated expertise across functions.

Assessing Climate
Risk in Line with
TCFD
In alignment with regulatory
guidance, including the Reserve
Bank of India’s discussion paper

304

on climate risk and sustainable
finance, your Company is
progressing towards adopting
a Task Force on Climate-related
Financial Disclosures aligned
framework. This includes
integrating climate considerations
across governance, strategy,
risk management, and metrics
and targets, while capturing the
interlinkages of climate risks and
opportunities.
For more information on your
company TCFD alignment refer to
'TCFD index'

and measurement of climate-related
risks through portfolio heatmapping
and physical risk assessments. This
exercise identifies concentrations
of risk and areas of heightened
vulnerability, providing granular
insights into exposure across
collateralised retail portfolios.
This analysis, supported by
external data sources evaluates
the likelihood and severity of key
hazards, including coastal and
riverine floods, cyclones, extreme
heat, landslides, and water scarcity,
at a highly granular level.

Recognising the increasing materiality of climate risks, LTF has advanced the integration of climate scenario analysis into its
emerging risk management framework. This forward-looking approach enables your Company to assess potential impacts
under different climate pathways across short, medium, and long-term horizons.
Your Company has considered multiple Representative Concentration Pathway scenarios for this analysis:
In FY26, two climate scenarios were assessed:
RCP 2.6, representing a low-emission pathway aligned with strong climate action and
accelerated mitigation efforts
RCP 4.5, reflecting a stabilisation scenario with moderate policy intervention
and adaptation
RCP 8.5, representing a high-emission scenario with more severe climate impacts

Your Company’s efforts towards emission reduction and sequestration of residual emissions remain aligned with its carbon
neutrality goal for 2035. These metrics and initiatives support informed decision-making, strengthen accountability, and
ensure alignment with evolving global best practices in climate action.

Managing Emissions
For a lending-led financial institution,
emissions management is intrinsically
linked to long-term portfolio resilience
and responsible growth. While LTF’s
direct operational emissions are
limited, the most significant climaterelated risks and impacts arise from its
lending portfolio and value chain. Your
Company understands that climate
action is fundamental to long-term
business resilience. We believe that
proactive emissions management is a
dual-purpose strategy: it significantly
lessens our environmental footprint
while simultaneously driving
operational efficiency.
Your Company is also expanding
financed emissions coverage in
line with evolving methodologies.
At the same time, it is enhancing
data automation to support more

robust measurement and disclosure.
These initiatives help embed climate
considerations into portfolio growth
and risk management.
Accordingly, your Company adopts an
integrated approach that:
Measures emissions across Scope 1,
Scope 2 and Scope 3 category
Prioritises financed emissions as a
key area of focus
Your Company has set a target
to achieve carbon neutrality for
Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions
by 2035. Emissions are measured
and disclosed in line with the GHG
Protocol Corporate Standard and the
Partnership for Carbon Accounting
Financials (PCAF) framework, ensuring
transparency and consistency.

Simultaneously, your Company is
charting its transition toward Net Zero
by identifying key decarbonization
levers and evaluating its alignment
with the Science Based Targets initiative
(SBTi). This ongoing planning phase
focuses on assessing the feasibility of
committing to formal, science-based
targets over the coming years to ensure
our long-term climate strategy remains
robust and globally credible.

20%
Reduction in scope 1+2 emissions

By taking solar to the edges of its network, LTF is demonstrating that meaningful climate action can be scaled across
every level of operations.

Scope 1 emissions arise from sources directly controlled by your Company. While LTF’s core financial services operations

During FY26, LTF strengthened operational controls across its expanding branch network and facilities to minimise
direct emissions. This included expanding green power adoption across branches, installing EV charging infrastructure,
piloting smart meters and automated energy data collection. In addition, preventive maintenance was enhanced
through efficiency upgrades such as LED-based signage, STP revamp, and continued process digitisation. To enhance
environmental stewardship, your Company has implemented rigorous maintenance protocols for its owned vehicles
and diesel generators while transitioning to low-GWP (Global Warming Potential) refrigerants, such as R32, to replace
legacy R22 models. By centralizing air conditioning maintenance under a single specialized vendor, your Company
has streamlined oversight and ensured consistent performance across all facilities. Together, these proactive measures
minimize your Company's impact on ozone depletion and climate change, ensuring strict regulatory compliance. Together,
these measures supported reductions in carbon intensity while ensuring operational continuity across locations.

Corporate Overview

Scope 2 Emissions

Statutory Reports

Financial Statements

Scope 3 Emissions

I

Indirect Emissions from Energy Consumption

II III

Scope 2 emissions represent indirect emissions associated with purchased electricity consumed across offices, and
branches, and data centres. Scope 2 emissions reflect electricity consumption across offices, branches, and data centres
and are influenced by the energy management initiatives described earlier in this chapter.
Your Company has actively accelerated its transition to clean energy, successfully meeting 100% of the electricity
requirements for its Head Office and 61 branches through green power. To ensure comprehensive accountability, your
Company adopts both location-based and market-based approaches for calculating Scope 2 emissions. While the locationbased method reflects grid consumption at operational sites, the market-based method highlights the positive impact of
renewable energy procurement, providing a transparent and nuanced view of your Company’s carbon footprint in line
with global best practices.
Recognizing the operational constraints of small, leased branches without dedicated metering, where direct renewable
procurement is not feasible, your Company bridged this gap by leveraging International Renewable Energy Certificates
(iRECs). These certificates, sourced from verified Indian projects and compliant with RE100 criteria, allow your Company to
claim green attributes for its total electricity consumption.
Beyond procurement, your Company has invested in on-site generation through solar rooftop PV projects at six rural
branches. Complementing these energy transitions are significant infrastructure upgrades, including the deployment of
IoT devices, LED lighting, and 5-star rated ACs. Collectively, these initiatives led to a ~8% tCO2e reduction in Scope 2
emissions. Notably, without your Company's dedicated efforts to secure green power and iRECs, Scope 2 emissions would
have reached ~7,335 tCO2e under the location-based assessment.

Scope 3 emissions encompass indirect activities across your Company’s broader value chain, including capital goods
procurement, employee commuting, business travel, and financed emissions linked to the lending portfolio. Recognizing
that these represent a significant portion of the total environmental impact, your Company initiated strategic measures in
FY26 to mitigate these emissions.
